threats.title: Guard blocklist omission allows theft of rented NFTs via permit() and non-standard transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: The Guard policy uses a blocklist approach to prevent NFT transfers, but fails to block EIP-4494 permit() and other non-standard transfer selectors. A renter can call permit() to grant an external address allowance to their rented NFT, bypassing the Guard, and then use the external address to transfer the NFT out of the Safe.
threats.investigation_rationale: Blocklist-based security on smart contract wallets is inherently fragile. Validating whether permit() or other custom transfer functions can successfully bypass the Guard will confirm if renters can easily steal rented assets without triggering the blocked selectors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Selector blocklist omits EIP-4494 permit (0x56044225) and custom transfer functions

preconditions: Rented NFT implements EIP-4494 permit() or a non-standard transfer function
Renter has execution rights on the rental Safe

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Guard only checks 10 specific function selectors
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter executes permit() via Safe, which is not blocked, granting allowance to an attacker EOA
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker EOA calls transferFrom() to steal the NFT, resulting in 100% loss of lender's asset value

economic_questions: Which popular NFT collections support permit or custom transfers?
Can the stolen NFT be sold immediately for profit?
validation_requirements: Test executing a permit() call through the Guard on a mock EIP-4494 NFT
Verify the external EOA can successfully pull the NFT

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard gas costs for two transactions (~100k gas)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of the rented NFT

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time during the active rental period

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rented EIP-4494 NFTs in the protocol

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Guard selector list vs standard NFT ABIs

threats.title: RentalWallet omission in order hash allows cross-wallet state corruption and ransom
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function omits the rentalWallet field from the EIP-712 encoding. An attacker can call stopRent for a victim's order but substitute their own Safe as the rentalWallet. This deletes the victim's order from storage but leaves the victim's Safe with a positive rentedAssets count, permanently bricking the victim's Safe from transferring any tokens of that ID.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cryptographic hash collisions due to omitted fields allow state desynchronization. Validating this requires proving that stopRent accepts the substituted wallet, successfully reclaims from the attacker's wallet, and leaves the victim's wallet permanently locked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode omits order.rentalWallet between order.renter and order.startTimestamp

preconditions: Victim has an active rental in Safe A
Attacker controls Safe B and deposits an identical NFT (e.g., same ERC1155 ID) into it

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Hash computation ignores the wallet holding the assets
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker stops victim's order using attacker's wallet, decrementing attacker's rentedAssets instead of victim's
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Victim's Safe is permanently locked for that token ID. Attacker can extort the victim for a ransom to provide a custom recovery contract (if delegatecalls are whitelisted) or simply grief them.

economic_questions: Can this be weaponized to steal funds, or is it purely griefing/ransom?
How much value can be locked in a single attack?
validation_requirements: Construct two RentalOrder structs differing only in rentalWallet
Verify they produce the same hash
Execute stopRent with the substituted wallet and verify state corruption

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of acquiring one identical NFT (for ERC1155)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires identical NFT available on market
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: While victim holds significant other assets of the same ID in their Safe
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe loss of user trust and locked funds

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Renters holding multiple units of an ERC1155 token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Permanent lock of all tokens of that ID in their Safe
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: High, especially for valuable gaming assets

threats.title: Order metadata hash omission allows event spoofing and off-chain manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveOrderMetadataHash function omits the emittedExtraData field. A fulfiller can intercept a signed Seaport order and modify the emittedExtraData before submitting it to the Create policy. This allows the attacker to inject arbitrary data into the RentalOrderStarted event without invalidating the lender's signature.
threats.investigation_rationale: While on-chain state is protected by structural item checks, off-chain indexers, frontends, and reward systems relying on the emittedExtraData can be manipulated. Validating this requires checking if the protocol's off-chain infrastructure trusts this event data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode omits metadata.emittedExtraData despite it being in the type string

preconditions: Protocol or third parties use emittedExtraData for off-chain logic (e.g., affiliate tracking, rewards)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Fulfiller modifies the RentPayload to include malicious extraData
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can steal affiliate fees, manipulate off-chain reward distributions, or spoof frontend displays

economic_questions: What off-chain systems consume the RentalOrderStarted event?
Is there direct financial value tied to emittedExtraData?
validation_requirements: Modify emittedExtraData in a signed payload and verify it passes validateOrder
Analyze off-chain indexer code if available

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI if rewards are exploitable

threats.title: Whitelisting batching contracts for delegatecall enables complete Guard bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: If the Admin whitelists a batching contract like MultiSend to allow Safe owners to batch transactions, the Guard is completely bypassed. The Safe can delegatecall MultiSend, which then makes standard calls to NFT contracts. Because these calls originate from within the delegatecall context, the Guard's checkTransaction is never triggered for the internal transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Gnosis Safe guards only intercept top-level execTransaction calls. If delegatecall is allowed to a contract that makes external calls, the guard is blind to them. Validating this requires confirming if MultiSend is whitelisted in production.
deployment_assumptions: Admin whitelists MultiSend or a similar batching contract to improve Safe usability
threats.config_evidence: Admin.sol exposes toggleWhitelistDelegate, intended for usability upgrades

proof_of_signal.pattern: Allows Enum.Operation.DelegateCall if STORE.whitelistedDelegates(to) is true

preconditions: MultiSend or similar contract is added to whitelistedDelegates
Renter wants to steal rented NFTs

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Guard assumes delegatecalled contracts are safe, but MultiSend executes arbitrary calls
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter crafts a MultiSend payload containing transferFrom calls for the rented NFTs
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals the NFTs while the protocol still considers them rented, resulting in 100% loss for lenders

economic_questions: Is MultiSend currently whitelisted on mainnet deployments?
Are there other whitelisted delegates that allow arbitrary calls?
validation_requirements: Whitelist MultiSend in a test environment
Execute a delegatecall to MultiSend that transfers a rented NFT
Verify the Guard fails to block the transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of all rented NFTs in the Safe

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a valuable NFT is rented

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets in Safes where MultiSend is whitelisted

threats.title: State-dependent hooks can be manipulated to DoS stopRent, locking lender assets
threats.hypothesis_description: The stopRent function calls _removeHooks, which executes IHook(target).onStop(). If the hook reverts, the entire stopRent transaction reverts. A malicious renter can manipulate the external state that the hook depends on (e.g., a staking contract) to ensure the hook always reverts, permanently preventing the rental from being stopped and keeping the NFT in their Safe.
threats.investigation_rationale: Hooks introduce external dependencies into the critical path of asset recovery. Validating this requires analyzing the specific hook implementations approved by the protocol to see if their success depends on renter-controlled state.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol approves hooks that interact with external state (e.g., staking/unstaking) which can fail based on user actions
threats.config_evidence: Guard.sol exposes updateHookStatus, allowing arbitrary external contracts to be added to the critical path

proof_of_signal.pattern: try IHook(target).onStop() catches errors and explicitly reverts the transaction

preconditions: An active rental uses a hook
The hook's onStop function can revert based on external state controlled by the renter

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Asset recovery is strictly dependent on external hook success
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter alters external state (e.g., withdraws all liquidity from a pool the hook checks) so onStop reverts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Renter retains indefinite use of the rented NFT without paying additional rent, effectively stealing its utility value

economic_questions: What specific hooks are currently approved?
Do any approved hooks have revert conditions tied to renter actions?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call stopRent and verify it fails

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Ongoing utility value of the NFT

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of asset control for lenders

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Permanent loss of access to their NFT
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value of all NFTs using the vulnerable hook

threats.title: Fee-on-transfer tokens cause Escrow insolvency and permanent lock of rented assets
threats.hypothesis_description: If a fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is used for rental payments, the PaymentEscrow records the full nominal amount in balanceOf[token], but receives a smaller actual amount. During settlePayment, the contract attempts to transfer the full nominal amount. This will eventually revert due to insufficient balance, permanently preventing the stopping of rentals and locking the associated NFTs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Accounting mismatches in escrow contracts lead to systemic insolvency. Validating this requires checking if the protocol actively blocks fee-on-transfer tokens at the Seaport level or if users can freely create orders with them.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol does not strictly whitelist ERC20 tokens, allowing users to create rentals with fee-on-transfer tokens
threats.config_evidence: No token whitelist is present in Create.sol; any ERC20 can be used in Seaport orders

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf[token] += amount without checking actual balance delta

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is used in a rental order
Multiple rentals use the same token, or the token is skimmed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: balanceOf tracks nominal amount, not actual received amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker creates a rental with a fee-on-transfer token, causing the contract to owe more than it holds
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can intentionally brick the escrow for a specific token, locking all NFTs rented with that token for griefing purposes

economic_questions: Can users freely choose payment tokens?
Is there a way to rescue locked NFTs if the escrow reverts?
validation_requirements: Stop the first rental (succeeds, draining the contract)
Attempt to stop the second rental (reverts due to insufficient balance)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Systemic DoS for specific payment tokens

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All NFTs rented with the affected token

threats.title: Active hooks completely bypass Guard protections, allowing asset theft
threats.hypothesis_description: When a hook is active for a target contract, Guard.checkTransaction forwards the call to the hook and completely skips its own _checkTransaction logic. If the Admin whitelists a hook that does not independently implement the strict selector blocklist, the renter can transfer rented assets out of the Safe.
threats.investigation_rationale: Delegating core security checks to external middleware is highly risky. Validating this requires auditing all approved hook contracts to ensure they perfectly replicate the Guard's blocklist.
deployment_assumptions: Admin deploys and whitelists a hook that focuses on specific logic (e.g., staking) but forgets to block standard NFT transfer selectors
threats.config_evidence: Guard.sol updateHookPath allows setting hooks for any target

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) skips the else block containing _checkTransaction

preconditions: Admin has whitelisted a hook for a target contract (e.g., the NFT contract itself)
The hook's onTransaction does not revert on transferFrom calls

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Complete bypass of base security checks when middleware is present
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter calls transferFrom on the target contract; hook allows it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Renter steals the NFT, resulting in 100% loss for the lender

economic_questions: Do they implement the same selector blocklist as the Guard?
validation_requirements: Review source code of all production hooks
Test transferring an NFT while a permissive hook is active

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: While the permissive hook is active

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lenders of assets associated with the hook
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets interacting with the vulnerable hook
